exercises.title: Identify checked vs unchecked
exercises.instructions:
For each exception, write C (checked) or U (unchecked):

1. `IOException`
2. `NullPointerException`
3. `SQLException`
4. `NumberFormatException`
5. `FileNotFoundException`
6. `IllegalArgumentException`
hints: Extends RuntimeException → Unchecked
Extends Exception (not via RuntimeException) → Checked
FileNotFoundException extends IOException

exercises.title: throws keyword syntax
exercises.scenario: Declare a checked exception in method signature.
exercises.instructions: Fill in the blank to correctly declare that this method can throw IOException.

blanks.hint: Keyword to declare checked exception in method signature

hints: `throws` (not `throw`) in the method signature
Position: after parameter list, before opening brace

exercises.title: Simulate and handle IOException
exercises.scenario: Work with a method that throws a checked exception.
exercises.instructions:
Write a method `openFile(String name)` that throws IOException when name starts with 'bad'. In main, handle the IOException.

Expected output:
bad-data.csv not found
good-data.csv opened
exercises.starter_code:
import java.io.*;

// openFile throws IOException for 'bad' files; catch in main
hints: Method signature: static void openFile(String name) throws IOException
throw new IOException(message) inside method
try-catch IOException in caller

exercises.title: Fix missing throws declaration
exercises.scenario: Checked exception not declared — compile error.
exercises.instructions:
This code won't compile. Fix it by adding the correct throws declaration. Don't add a try-catch — propagate the exception.

Expected: compile and run successfully.

bugs.issue: readFirstLine calls FileReader and readLine() — both throw checked IOException. Method must declare throws IOException.
bugs.fix: Add `throws IOException` to the method signature

hints: FileReader() throws FileNotFoundException (extends IOException)
readLine() throws IOException
Solution: add `throws IOException` after the parameter list

exercises.title: Propagate vs catch — two versions
exercises.scenario: Same operation implemented both ways: catch and declare throws.
exercises.instructions:
Implement `loadConfig` TWO ways:
1. `loadConfigCatch(String name)` — catches IOException, returns default message
2. `loadConfigThrows(String name)` — declares throws IOException, propagates

Show both called from main — one handles inline, one needs try-catch in main.

Expected output:
Version 1: Config load failed, using defaults
Version 2: caught in main: missing.cfg unavailable
exercises.starter_code:
import java.io.*;

// Two versions: catch internally vs declare throws

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Version 1: Config load failed, using defaults
Version 2: caught in main: missing.cfg unavailable

hints: Version 1: no throws keyword, catch inside
Version 2: throws IOException in signature, no catch
main needs try-catch for version 2

exercises.title: Throw IllegalArgumentException (unchecked)
exercises.scenario: Throw unchecked exception for invalid input — no throws declaration needed.
exercises.instructions:
Write `createUser(String name, int age)` that throws IllegalArgumentException (unchecked) if:
- name is null or blank: 'Name cannot be blank'
- age < 0 or > 150: 'Invalid age: [age]'

Note: no throws declaration needed (it's unchecked).

Expected output:
User: Aisha, 28
IllegalArgumentException: Name cannot be blank
IllegalArgumentException: Invalid age: 200
exercises.starter_code: // createUser validates and throws unchecked IllegalArgumentException

test_cases.expected_stdout:
User: Aisha, 28
IllegalArgumentException: Name cannot be blank
IllegalArgumentException: Invalid age: 200

hints: No throws declaration needed — IllegalArgumentException is unchecked
throw new IllegalArgumentException(message) inside method
Callers can optionally catch (no compiler requirement)

exercises.title: Multiple throws declarations
exercises.scenario: Method can throw multiple checked exceptions.
exercises.instructions:
Write `processPayment(String amountStr, String filePath)` that:
- Parses amount (throws NumberFormatException — unchecked, no declaration needed)
- Simulates logging to file (throws IOException — checked, MUST declare)
- Both errors should propagate to caller

Expected output:
₹5000.0 logged to payments.log
NumberFormatException caught: For input string: "abc"
IOException caught: payments-bad.log write failed

test_cases.expected_stdout:
₹5000.0 logged to payments.log
NumberFormatException caught: For input string: "abc"
IOException caught: payments-bad.log write failed

hints: Only IOException needs to be declared in throws — NFE is unchecked
Caller catches NFE first (more specific), then IOException (broader)
NFE from Double.parseDouble occurs before the file write

exercises.title: Wrap checked in unchecked — Spring pattern
exercises.scenario: Convert infrastructure exception to domain exception.
exercises.instructions:
Implement the Spring-style exception wrapping pattern:
1. `DataRepository.findUser(int id)` throws checked `SQLException` when id < 0
2. `UserService.getUser(int id)` catches SQLException and rethrows as unchecked `DataAccessException`
3. `UserService.getUser()` can be called without try-catch

Expected output:
User found: Aisha
DataAccessException: Database error for id=-1 — caused by: negative id

test_cases.expected_stdout:
User found: Aisha
DataAccessException: Database error for id=-1 — caused by: negative id

hints: DataAccessException extends RuntimeException — unchecked
UserService catches SQLException (checked), rethrows as DataAccessException (unchecked)
No throws declaration needed on getUser()
e.getCause() retrieves the original SQLException

exercises.title: Throws propagates up the call chain
exercises.scenario: Throws declaration must propagate or be caught at each level.
exercises.expected_solution:
Caught at level1: deep failure
After level1

test_cases.expected:
Caught at level1: deep failure
After level1

hints: level3 throws IOException
level2 declares throws IOException — passes responsibility up
level1 catches IOException — caught here
main sees normal execution after level1 returns

exercises.title: Three-layer exception architecture
exercises.instructions:
Implement three classes:
- `Repository.fetch(String id)` — throws checked `DataException` when id='MISSING'
- `Service.process(String id)` — wraps DataException in unchecked `ServiceException`
- `Controller.handleRequest(String id)` — catches ServiceException, returns user message

Expected output:
Result: Data for ID-001
Error: Data unavailable — please retry

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Result: Data for ID-001
Error: Data unavailable — please retry

hints: DataException extends Exception — checked, needs throws or catch
ServiceException extends RuntimeException — unchecked
Service translates DataException (infrastructure) to ServiceException (domain)
Controller is the final boundary — handles ServiceException for the user

exercises.title: Exception hierarchy instanceof check
exercises.scenario: Use hierarchy to catch parent and identify child.
exercises.instructions:
Catch IOException in one block and identify whether it's a FileNotFoundException (subtype) or a general IOException. Use instanceof.

Expected output:
FileNotFoundException: missing.txt (No such file or directory)
General IOException: network error
exercises.starter_code:
import java.io.*;

// Catch IOException, identify whether it's FileNotFoundException using instanceof

test_cases.expected_stdout:
FileNotFoundException: missing.txt (No such file or directory)
General IOException: network error

hints: FileNotFoundException extends IOException — one catch block catches both
Use instanceof to distinguish at runtime
Pattern matching: if (e instanceof FileNotFoundException fnfe)

exercises.title: Checked exception in multi-step pipeline
exercises.scenario: Multiple checked exceptions in a data processing pipeline.
exercises.instructions:
Simulate an import pipeline: validate → parse → save. Each step can fail with a checked ProcessingException.

```java
class ProcessingException extends Exception { ... }
```

Run three records through: one succeeds, one fails validation, one fails parsing.

Expected output:
Record 1: imported
Record 2: FAILED — validation: Name required
Record 3: FAILED — parsing: Invalid date format
exercises.starter_code: // Import pipeline with checked ProcessingException per step

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Record 1: imported
Record 2: FAILED — validation: Name required
Record 3: FAILED — parsing: Invalid date format

hints: ProcessingException extends Exception — checked
importRecord declares throws ProcessingException
Caller catches it for each record individually — other records continue

exercises.title: Library API with correct checked/unchecked design
exercises.scenario: Design exceptions appropriately for a public API.
exercises.instructions:
Design a `BookLibrary` class with proper exception design:
- `addBook(Book)` — throws unchecked `IllegalArgumentException` if book is null or duplicate ISBN
- `checkout(String isbn, String patron)` — throws checked `BookUnavailableException` if book doesn't exist or already checked out
- `getBook(String isbn)` — throws unchecked `IllegalArgumentException` if isbn is null

Expected output:
Added: Clean Code (ISBN-001)
IllegalArgumentException: Book cannot be null
Checked out: Clean Code to Aisha
BookUnavailableException: ISBN-001 is already checked out
Found: Clean Code
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.*;

// BookLibrary with correct checked/unchecked exception design

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Added: Clean Code (ISBN-001)
IllegalArgumentException: Book cannot be null
Checked out: Clean Code to Aisha
BookUnavailableException: ISBN-001 is already checked out
Found: Clean Code

hints: IllegalArgumentException is unchecked — no throws declaration
BookUnavailableException extends Exception — checked, needs throws on checkout()
checkout throws BookUnavailableException — 'book might not be available' is an EXPECTED outcome in a library system

exercises.title: Simulate JDBC-style checked exception flow
exercises.scenario: Real JDBC pattern — checked SQL exceptions through layers.
exercises.instructions:
Simulate a JDBC-style data access:
- `QueryRunner.execute(String sql)` throws checked `QueryException` for bad SQL
- `UserDAO.findByName(String name)` uses QueryRunner, wraps QueryException in unchecked `DAOException`
- `UserService.lookupUser(String name)` calls UserDAO — no try-catch needed

Test finding existing and missing users.

Expected output:
Found: User[Aisha,aisha@x.com]
DAOException: DAO failed for query: name='BADUSER' — Simulated: record not found
exercises.starter_code: // JDBC-style layered exception: QueryException → DAOException wrapping

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Found: User[Aisha,aisha@x.com]
DAOException: DAO failed for query: name='BADUSER' — Simulated: record not found

hints: QueryException is checked (extends Exception)
DAOException is unchecked (extends RuntimeException)
UserDAO wraps QueryException in DAOException
UserService has no try-catch — DAOException propagates naturally

exercises.title: Design full exception hierarchy for a payment system
exercises.scenario: Complete exception design for a real-world domain.
exercises.instructions:
Design a payment exception hierarchy:

```
PaymentException (checked — extends Exception)
├── InsufficientFundsException (checked — has: accountId, available, attempted)
└── InvalidPaymentException (unchecked — extends RuntimeException)
    ├── InvalidAmountException  (zero or negative amount)
    └── InvalidAccountException (unknown account)
```

Implement `PaymentProcessor.transfer(from, to, amount)` that throws appropriate exceptions. Process 4 transfers demonstrating each exception type.

Expected output:
Transferred ₹1000.0: A001→A002
InvalidAmountException: Amount must be positive, got: -500.0
InvalidAccountException: Account not found: A999
InsufficientFundsException: A001 has ₹4000.0, needed ₹5000.0
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.*;

// Full payment exception hierarchy and processor

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Transferred ₹1000.0: A001→A002
InvalidAmountException: Amount must be positive, got: -500.0
InvalidAccountException: Account not found: A999
InsufficientFundsException: A001 has ₹4000.0, needed ₹5000.0

hints: PaymentException is checked — transfer declares `throws PaymentException`
InvalidPaymentException is unchecked — doesn't need throws
After first transfer: A001 has 4000 (started 5000, sent 1000)
Catch specific types first (InsufficientFundsException before PaymentException)
